one_line_takeaway: MCP's own protocol design -- self-asserted capabilities, unauthenticated 'user'-role sampling, and no cross-server isolation -- independently amplifies prompt-injection attack success by 23-41 percentage points over equivalent non-MCP tool integrations, and the authors' ATTESTMCP extension (capability attestation + HMAC message auth + origin tagging + user-gated cross-server flow) cuts overall ASR from 52.8% to 12.4% for about 8ms of added latency.
key_finding: MCP's architecture amplifies overall attack success rate from 26.4% (non-MCP baseline) to 52.8% (MCP), a +26.4 percentage-point increase, with per-attack-type amplification ranging +16.6 to +41.6 points: Indirect Injection (Resource) 31.2%->47.8% (+16.6%), Tool Response Manipulation 28.4%->52.1% (+23.7%), Cross-Server Propagation 19.7%->61.3% (+41.6%, the largest amplification), and Sampling-Based Injection 67.2% (no non-MCP equivalent exists). Sampling-specific attacks succeeded 58.3% (Claude-3.5-Sonnet) to 72.1% (Llama-3.1-70B) of the time, with 42.1-61.2% exfiltration rates and 31.7-44.6% session-persistence rates; Claude-3.5-Sonnet was the most resilient model tested. ASR scaled with server count in multi-server trust exploitation: 47.8% (1 server) -> 58.4% (2, cascade rate 34.2%) -> 67.1% (3, cascade 51.8%) -> 78.3% (5, cascade 72.4%). A system-prompt-only mitigation ('never pass data between servers without user confirmation') reduced cross-server ASR from 61.3% to 47.2% (a 23% relative reduction), which the authors argue shows prompt-level defenses are insufficient. Comparing against prior published benchmark numbers when re-run through MCP infrastructure: InjecAgent on GPT-4 rose from a reported 24-48% to 51.2% under MCP; AgentDojo best-agent from <25% to 38.7%; Agent-SafetyBench safety score from <60% to 47.3%. ATTESTMCP reduced overall ASR from 52.8% to 12.4% (76.5% relative reduction), with the largest per-category reductions in Cross-Server Propagation (61.3%->8.7%, 85.8% reduction) and Sampling-Based Injection (67.2%->11.3%, 83.2% reduction); Indirect Injection fell 47.8%->18.4% (61.5% reduction) and Tool Response Manipulation 52.1%->14.2% (72.7% reduction). Median per-message overhead was 8.3ms cold (P95 14.2ms, P99 21.7ms) and 2.4ms warm-cache (P95 4.1ms, P99 6.2ms), judged negligible against typical LLM inference latency (500-2000ms).
core_contribution: The paper presents a security analysis of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) specification (v1.0, Dec 2024) itself, arguing that MCP's architectural choices amplify prompt-injection and tool-abuse attacks beyond what non-MCP tool integrations exhibit. It identifies three protocol-level vulnerabilities: (1) unverified, self-asserted capability declarations at initialization that allow capability escalation with no enforcement mechanism (violates least privilege); (2) the sampling/createMessage mechanism, which lets servers request LLM completions using a 'user' role with no origin authentication, so injected server content is indistinguishable from real user input (the paper surveyed three MCP hosts -- Claude Desktop 1.2.3, Cursor 0.44, Continue 0.9 -- and found none provide a visual indicator distinguishing sampling-derived messages); and (3) implicit trust propagation across servers in multi-server deployments, where the spec defines no isolation boundary, so a compromised Server A can influence tool calls on Server B or exfiltrate Server B's data through Server A's channel. To measure these effects empirically the authors built PROTOAMP, a framework that wraps existing agent-security benchmark tasks (InjecAgent, AgentDojo) as MCP-compliant servers and adds injection points at three protocol layers (resource content, tool response payloads, sampling requests), then compares attack success rate (ASR) against a matched non-MCP baseline (direct function calls with simulated network latency) using identical tool semantics and injection payloads. They ran 847 attack scenarios (312 InjecAgent adaptations, 398 AgentDojo adaptations, 137 novel protocol-specific attacks) across five MCP server implementations (filesystem, git, sqlite, slack, and a custom adversarial-mcp server) with three LLM backends (Claude-3.5-Sonnet, GPT-4o, Llama-3.1-70B). They also propose ATTESTMCP, a backward-compatible protocol extension adding cryptographic capability attestation (signed certificates from a capability authority), HMAC-SHA256 message authentication, sampling origin tagging, user-authorized cross-server isolation, and nonce/timestamp replay protection, and re-ran the full evaluation with a client-side shim implementing it.
threat_model: Adversary controls or compromises one MCP server within a multi-server deployment, or can inject content into external data sources (web pages, documents) that servers retrieve; adversary has black-box access only (cannot modify LLM weights or host application code). Adversary goals: hijacking agent behavior, exfiltrating sensitive context/data, and persisting malicious state across sessions. The paper also separately catalogs how malicious servers reach users in practice, based on a survey of 127 MCP server installation guides: typosquatting (34%, e.g. 'mcp-server-filesytem'), supply-chain compromise via dependency poisoning (28%), social engineering (23%, with 73% of surveyed guides instructing users to run npx directly against GitHub URLs with no integrity verification), and marketplace poisoning in IDE extension stores (15%).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) ATTESTMCP does not address attacks from a single legitimately-authorized server serving malicious content, social engineering of users into authorizing malicious capabilities, or CA compromise (mitigated but not eliminated by federation); (2) it provides no protection on first contact due to Trust-On-First-Use pinning -- a malicious server that never claims ATTESTMCP support is not caught; (3) if most servers stay unsigned/legacy, users will default to 'Permissive' mode, negating the security benefit; (4) the residual 12.4% ASR largely reflects indirect injection via legitimately-retrieved content, which the authors say is not solvable at the protocol layer; (5) ASR measurements assume users carefully review cross-server authorization prompts, but the authors flag alert fatigue as a likely real-world confound and call for future user studies; (6) only five MCP servers were tested, and production deployments with dozens of servers may behave differently; (7) ATTESTMCP has not been formally verified (symbolic model checking left to future work); (8) the federated CA trust model requires ecosystem coordination that may face adoption barriers; (9) the authors did not evaluate adversarial attempts to bypass ATTESTMCP itself.

datasets_used: 137 novel protocol-specific attack scenarios (sampling, cross-server) authored by the paper
Survey of 127 MCP server installation guides for the server-discovery attack-vector analysis
quantitative_results: Overall ASR: 26.4% baseline vs 52.8% MCP (+26.4 pts)
Sampling attack ASR by model: Claude-3.5-Sonnet 58.3%, GPT-4o 71.4%, Llama-3.1-70B 72.1%
ASR by compromised-server count: 47.8% (1 server), 58.4%/34.2% cascade (2), 67.1%/51.8% cascade (3), 78.3%/72.4% cascade (5)
Isolation-utility tradeoff: none/MCP default 61.3% ASR / 94.2% task completion; user-prompted 31.7% ASR / 87.4% task completion; strict 8.7% ASR / 61.8% task completion
System-prompt-only mitigation: cross-server ASR 61.3% -> 47.2% (23% relative reduction), still far above ATTESTMCP's 8.7%
Prior-benchmark re-run under MCP: InjecAgent (GPT-4) 24-48% (original) -> 51.2% (MCP); AgentDojo (best agent) <25% -> 38.7%; Agent-SafetyBench (safety score) <60% -> 47.3%

ATTESTMCP defense effectiveness: overall ASR 52.8% -> 12.4% (76.5% reduction); Indirect Injection 47.8%->18.4% (61.5%); Tool Response Manipulation 52.1%->14.2% (72.7%); Cross-Server Propagation 61.3%->8.7% (85.8%); Sampling-Based Injection 67.2%->11.3% (83.2%)
ATTESTMCP latency: median 8.3ms cold / 2.4ms warm per message (P95 14.2/4.1ms, P99 21.7/6.2ms)
MCP transport latency measured: median 12.4ms (stdio), 23.7ms (HTTP/SSE); latency effect on refusal rate not significant (paired t-test p>0.3, n=200)
Server-discovery vectors from 127-guide survey: typosquatting 34%, supply-chain compromise 28%, social engineering 23% (73% of guides used unverified npx-from-GitHub), marketplace poisoning 15%
baselines_compared: Non-MCP baseline: equivalent tool integrations via direct function calls, with simulated network latency matched to MCP, identical tool semantics and injection payloads (used throughout Table IV/V/VI comparisons)
System-prompt-only mitigation ('never pass data between servers without confirmation') as a non-protocol-level defense baseline
Prior published benchmark numbers (InjecAgent on GPT-4, AgentDojo best agent, Agent-SafetyBench) re-executed through MCP infrastructure for comparison
Three isolation policy levels (none/user-prompted/strict) compared against each other on ASR and task completion
MCP (undefended) vs. ATTESTMCP (defended) as the before/after comparison for the proposed extension
